title: Apricot frangipane tart
subtitle: A classic French tart with almond cream, fresh apricots and an apricot glaze
excerpt: Fresh apricots halved and pressed cut-side up into almond frangipane, baked in a sweet pastry case. The fruit softens and caramelises around the almond cream as it bakes, and a brush of warm apricot jam glaze finishes it with a professional shine. This is a classic French patisserie tart. The total time, including chilling the pastry, is about 2 hours.
sourceNotes: Tarte aux abricots avec creme d'amande is documented in standard French patisserie texts including Auguste Escoffier's 'Le Guide Culinaire' (1903) and Gaston Lenotre's 'Lenotre's Desserts and Pastries' (1977). The apricot glaze technique is a standard French finish for fruit tarts, described in the same texts. Richard Bertinet gives a comprehensive method in 'Pastry' (2012).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The blind-baked case and frangipane can each be made a day ahead. Assemble and bake on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; blind-bake at 190 degrees C, then reduce to 180 degrees C for the filled tart

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin layer of warmed, sieved apricot jam brushed over a baked fruit tart while both are still warm. The pectin in the jam sets as the tart cools, creating a transparent layer that gives the surface a gloss, protects the fruit from oxidising and adds a mild sweetness. Warm the jam with a splash of water in a small pan until fluid, then pass through a fine sieve to remove lumps of fruit before applying with a pastry brush. Apply with light strokes; pressing hard into the fruit will dislodge it.

content.text: is the step that lifts this tart from a homemade bake to a patisserie-level finish. Apply it while the tart is still warm so it sets to a clear, glossy film. Once the tart has cooled fully, the glaze will not adhere properly.

Use ripe but firm apricots. Very ripe fruit collapses into the frangipane during baking, making the filling wet. If only soft apricots are available, use tinned apricots in juice, drained and thoroughly patted dry.

Rub the 100 g cold butter into the 200 g flour and 40 g icing sugar. Mix in the egg yolk and enough cold water to bring together into a dough. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin, prick the base, and blind-bake with baking beans for 15 minutes. Remove beans and paper, bake for 5 to 8 minutes more until the base is pale golden. Reduce oven to 180 degrees C.

Beat the 100 g softened butter and 100 g caster sugar until pale. Beat in 2 eggs one at a time. Fold in the 100 g ground almonds, 15 g flour and 0.5 tsp almond extract. Spread into the case. Press the 400 g apricot halves cut-side up into the frangipane in a single layer. Bake at 180 degrees C fan for 38 to 45 minutes until the frangipane is puffed, deep golden brown, and set with only a faint wobble at the very centre. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes.

Warm the 3 tbsp apricot jam with 1 tbsp water in a small pan until fluid. Pass through a sieve. Brush over the warm tart with light strokes. Leave to set for 10 minutes, then remove the tin sides and cool completely before slicing.

items.problem: Apricots collapsed and the frangipane is wet
items.solution: The apricots were overripe. Fresh apricots should feel firm when halved. Tinned apricots are a reliable substitute: drain thoroughly and pat with kitchen paper before arranging in the tart.

items.problem: Glaze looks cloudy rather than clear
items.solution: The jam was not sieved. All fruit pulp must be removed before brushing; even a small amount of fruit pieces will cloud the glaze as it sets.

items.problem: Frangipane puffed over the sides of the pastry case
items.solution: The case was overfilled. The frangipane should fill the case to two thirds of its depth before the fruit is added, not more. It will rise to the top during baking.
